content.text: Packaging & Dependencies for Python Data Pipelines

Shipping a data pipeline from a developer's laptop into production is one of the most failure-prone steps in the entire machine learning and data engineering lifecycle. The code might work perfectly in a local environment, yet crash in staging because a library version differs by a single patch release, a system-level dependency is missing, or the Python interpreter itself is the wrong version. Packaging and dependency management is the discipline that eliminates this class of failure by making environments reproducible, portable, and auditable.

Packaging refers to bundling your pipeline's source code, metadata, and configuration into a distributable unit — a Python package, a container image, or an installable archive — such that it can be moved between machines and run identically. Dependency management is the practice of precisely specifying, resolving, and locking every external library your pipeline requires, from the direct dependencies you import explicitly to the transitive dependencies those libraries themselves need.
Together, these two practices answer the question:
"Given a blank machine, what is the exact set of instructions needed to reproduce the environment in which this pipeline was authored and tested?"
Data pipelines are uniquely sensitive to environment drift for several reasons.
A change in NumPy's linear algebra backend, or a minor version bump in pandas that silently alters how
handles NaN values, can change pipeline outputs without raising any errors. The bug is invisible until someone notices the model accuracy degraded or the financial report has different numbers.
A pipeline travels through dev → CI → staging → production → possibly multiple cloud regions. Each hop is an opportunity for the environment to diverge if versions are not locked.
When five engineers work on the same pipeline, each installs dependencies on different dates. Without locking, engineer A's
released a week later. Their test results cannot be directly compared.
A production scheduler (Airflow, Prefect, Argo) launching pipeline runs every hour must not discover that a newly released transitive dependency breaks execution at 3 AM.
Before addressing tooling, it is essential to understand the underlying mechanics Python uses to find and load packages.
— an ordered list of directories — and returns the first matching module it finds. The contents of
depend on how Python was invoked and what virtual environment, if any, is active. This is why "it works on my machine" problems almost always trace back to something about
directory within the Python installation. On a system Python this is a shared, global directory. A virtual environment creates an isolated copy of
, preventing conflicts between projects that require different versions of the same library.
Every package declares its own dependencies. When you install

. Each of those may have their own dependencies. This forms a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Resolving this graph means finding a single version of every node such that all version constraints from all packages are simultaneously satisfied — a constraint satisfaction problem that becomes genuinely hard for large pipelines with many dependencies.
versioning and use constraint operators in
In a lockfile, every dependency is pinned to an exact
version, including a cryptographic hash of the distribution artifact to prevent tampering.
A virtual environment is an isolated Python installation. It has its own
binary. Activating a virtual environment prepends its
resolve to the isolated copies rather than the system versions.

# Create
python3.11 -m venv .venv

# Activate (Linux/macOS)
source .venv/bin/activate

# Activate (Windows)
.venv\Scripts\activate.bat

# Confirm isolation
which python   # should show .venv/bin/python
pip list       # shows only the stdlib + pip itself
. It is a local artifact, not source code.
Why Not Use the System Python
The system Python on Linux distributions is often used by OS tools. Installing packages into it with
can break system utilities. Beyond that, the system Python version is fixed by the OS and may be several major versions behind what the pipeline requires.
The simplest format: one package per line, optionally with version constraints.

# requirements.txt (abstract dependencies — what you actually need)
pandas>=2.0,<3.0
numpy>=1.24
scikit-learn~=1.3.0
apache-airflow==2.8.1
) contains the full resolved and pinned output:

# requirements-frozen.txt (concrete, locked)
pandas==2.1.4
numpy==1.26.4
scikit-learn==1.3.2
joblib==1.3.2
threadpoolctl==3.2.0
# ... all transitive dependencies
: it does not record hashes, it does not distinguish direct from transitive dependencies, and it cannot separate development dependencies from production ones cleanly.
: Better Workflow on Top of pip
— the compiled, pinned lockfile generated from

pip install pip-tools
pip-compile requirements.in         # generates requirements.txt with hashes
pip-sync requirements.txt           # installs exactly what is in the lockfile

#
# This file is autogenerated by pip-compile with Python 3.11
# by the following command:
#
#    pip-compile requirements.in
#
joblib==1.3.2 \
    --hash=sha256:92f865e621e17784e7955080b6d042489e3b8cbb2f... \
    --hash=sha256:ef4...
pandas==2.1.4 \
    --hash=sha256:...
scikit-learn==1.3.2 \
    --hash=sha256:...
is strictly idempotent: it installs what is in the lockfile and
anything extra, guaranteeing the environment is an exact mirror of the spec.
is the current Python standard for declaring build system requirements and project metadata. It replaces

# pyproject.toml
[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

[project]
name = "sales-forecast-pipeline"
version = "1.0.0"
description = "Daily sales forecasting pipeline"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "pandas>=2.0,<3.0",
    "scikit-learn~=1.3",
    "sqlalchemy>=2.0",
    "psycopg2-binary>=2.9",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=7.0",
    "pytest-cov",
    "ruff",
    "mypy",
]
airflow = [
    "apache-airflow==2.8.1",
]

[project.scripts]
run-pipeline = "sales_pipeline.main:main"
dependencies (constraints, not pinned versions). The lockfile is separate and tool-specific.
Poetry is the most popular all-in-one tool that handles dependency resolution, virtual environment management, building, and publishing.

pip install poetry

# Start a new pipeline project
poetry new sales-pipeline
cd sales-pipeline

# Add dependencies
poetry add pandas "scikit-learn~=1.3"
poetry add --group dev pytest ruff

# Install from lockfile (for CI/production)
poetry install --no-root --only main
— fully resolved, pinned, hashed lockfile (committed to git):

[[package]]
name = "pandas"
version = "2.1.4"
description = "..."
python-versions = ">=3.9"

[package.dependencies]
numpy = {version = ">=1.23.2", python-version = ">=3.11"}
python-dateutil = ">=2.8.2"

[package.extras]
...

[metadata]
lock-version = "2.0"
python-versions = "^3.11"
content-hash = "..."
The lockfile is resolved once and committed. Every subsequent
produces exactly the same environment on every machine.

poetry install              # install all deps from lockfile
poetry install --only main  # skip dev deps (for production)
poetry update pandas        # upgrade one package, re-resolve, update lockfile
poetry export -f requirements.txt --output requirements.txt  # export for Docker
poetry run python pipeline/main.py  # run inside the managed venv
poetry build                # build a .whl distributable

is a newer tool written in Rust that is 10–100× faster than pip for dependency resolution and installation. It is designed as a drop-in replacement workflow for pip + pip-tools and has growing support for Poetry-style workflows.

pip install uv

# Create a virtual environment
uv venv .venv

# Install from requirements
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

# Compile a lockfile
uv pip compile requirements.in -o requirements.txt

# Sync the environment
uv pip sync requirements.txt

is especially valuable in CI pipelines where dependency installation time directly affects pipeline cost and developer feedback loop speed. On large dependency trees, what pip takes 60–90 seconds to install,
Conda is a language-agnostic package manager originally built for scientific Python. It manages both Python packages
system-level binary dependencies (C libraries, CUDA toolkits, MKL).

# environment.yml
name: sales-pipeline
channels:
  - conda-forge
  - defaults
dependencies:
  - python=3.11
  - numpy=1.26
  - pandas=2.1
  - scikit-learn=1.3
  - cudatoolkit=11.8      # system-level GPU dependency
  - pip:
    - apache-airflow==2.8.1  # not on conda, use pip inside conda env
conda env create -f environment.yml
conda activate sales-pipeline

# Lock the full environment
conda env export > environment-locked.yml
is a drop-in replacement for conda's solver, also written in C++ and significantly faster for resolving large scientific environments.

Conda is the right choice when the pipeline depends on compiled scientific libraries (PyTorch with CUDA, OpenCV, GDAL) where binary compatibility with system libraries matters and pip's wheel format is insufficient.
Packaging Your Pipeline as a Python Package

Treating a data pipeline as an installable Python package rather than a collection of scripts provides several structural advantages: it makes imports clean and absolute, enables CLI entry points, allows versioning, and integrates cleanly with container builds.

sales-pipeline/
├── pyproject.toml
├── poetry.lock
├── README.md
├── src/
│   └── sales_pipeline/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── main.py
│       ├── extract.py
│       ├── transform.py
│       ├── load.py
│       └── config.py
├── tests/
│   ├── conftest.py
│   ├── test_extract.py
│   └── test_transform.py
├── Dockerfile
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── ci.yml
layout is strongly preferred. It forces the package to be installed before it can be imported, which catches packaging issues in development that would otherwise only surface in production.

# pyproject.toml with src layout
[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
packages = ["src/sales_pipeline"]

# or with Poetry:
[tool.poetry]
packages = [{include = "sales_pipeline", from = "src"}]

poetry build
# or
python -m build   # using the build package with pyproject.toml

# Output:
# dist/sales_pipeline-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl
# dist/sales_pipeline-1.0.0.tar.gz
file is a zip archive of the compiled package. It can be installed with
on any compatible machine without needing the source code or a build step.

For production data pipelines, a Docker container is the gold standard for environment reproducibility. It packages Python, all dependencies, system libraries, and the application code into a single immutable artifact that runs identically from a developer laptop to a Kubernetes cluster.

# ---- Build stage ----
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /app

# Install Poetry
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir poetry==1.7.1

# Copy only dependency files first (layer caching)
COPY pyproject.toml poetry.lock ./

# Export to requirements.txt and install into /app/deps
RUN poetry export -f requirements.txt --without-hashes --only main \
    | pip install --no-cache-dir --target /app/deps -r /dev/stdin

# ---- Runtime stage ----
FROM python:3.11-slim AS runtime

# Security: run as non-root user
RUN useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash pipeline

WORKDIR /home/pipeline/app

# Copy installed deps from builder
COPY --from=builder /app/deps /app/deps
ENV PYTHONPATH=/app/deps

# Copy pipeline source
COPY --chown=pipeline:pipeline src/ ./src/
COPY --chown=pipeline:pipeline pyproject.toml ./

# Install the package itself (no deps, they're already in /app/deps)
RUN pip install --no-deps --no-cache-dir -e .

USER pipeline

ENTRYPOINT ["run-pipeline"]
The most important Docker optimization for pipeline builds is copying dependency files

# WRONG: source code changes invalidate the dep installation layer
COPY . .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

# CORRECT: dep layer is cached unless lockfile changes
COPY requirements.txt ./
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY src/ ./src/
With the correct ordering, a code change that doesn't touch
layer. Dependency installation (which can take minutes) is served from cache.
The builder stage can include compilers and build tools needed to compile C extensions. The runtime stage gets only the compiled artifacts, resulting in a dramatically smaller final image.

FROM python:3.11 AS builder   # Full image with gcc, build tools
RUN pip install numpy         # Compiles from source if needed

FROM python:3.11-slim AS runtime  # Minimal runtime (~50MB vs ~900MB)
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages
gets to ~50MB but can cause issues with compiled packages that assume glibc.
A real pipeline project has multiple environments with different dependency sets.

# pyproject.toml with Poetry
[tool.poetry.dependencies]          # production runtime deps
python = "^3.11"
pandas = ">=2.0"
scikit-learn = "~1.3"
psycopg2-binary = ">=2.9"

[tool.poetry.group.dev.dependencies]  # only for local development
pytest = ">=7.0"
pytest-cov = "*"
ruff = "*"
mypy = "*"
ipykernel = "*"  # Jupyter support

[tool.poetry.group.ci.dependencies]   # only in CI
pytest = ">=7.0"
pytest-cov = "*"

[tool.poetry.group.docs.dependencies]
sphinx = "*"
poetry install --only main   # skip dev, ci, docs groups
poetry install --with ci     # main + ci group
This prevents dev tools (linters, Jupyter) from bloating production images and avoids security exposure from unnecessary packages.

Dependencies and configuration are separate concerns, but they are often confused. Dependencies are code libraries. Configuration is runtime parameters (database URLs, API keys, thresholds). The twelve-factor app pattern mandates that configuration comes from environment variables, not baked into the image.
At runtime, inject via Docker or Kubernetes:

Organizations often publish internal helper libraries (shared ETL utilities, internal connectors, audit logging modules) to a private Python package registry. These are consumed exactly like public PyPI packages.
Configuring pip and Poetry for a Private Registry

# pyproject.toml (Poetry)
[[tool.poetry.source]]
name = "company-registry"
url = "https://my-company.d.codeartifact.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/pypi/python/simple/"
priority = "supplemental"  # check PyPI first, then fallback here

# pip.conf or environment variable approach
[global]
index-url = https://my-company.d.codeartifact.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/pypi/python/simple/
extra-index-url = https://pypi.org/simple/
In CI, the registry token is injected as an environment variable:

export CODEARTIFACT_AUTH_TOKEN=$(aws codeartifact get-authorization-token \
  --domain my-company --query authorizationToken --output text)
The lockfile is the contract between development and CI. CI must install from the lockfile, not re-resolve dependencies.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: Pipeline CI

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"

      - name: Cache Poetry dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/pypoetry
          key: poetry-${{ hashFiles('poetry.lock') }}
          restore-keys: poetry-

      - name: Install Poetry
        run: pip install poetry==1.7.1

      - name: Install dependencies (from lockfile)
        run: poetry install --no-root --with ci

      - name: Run linter
        run: poetry run ruff check src/ tests/

      - name: Run type checker
        run: poetry run mypy src/

      - name: Run tests with coverage
        run: poetry run pytest tests/ --cov=sales_pipeline --cov-report=xml

      - name: Build package
        run: poetry build

  build-docker:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: test
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build Docker image
        run: docker build -t sales-pipeline:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Run smoke test in container
        run: docker run --rm sales-pipeline:${{ github.sha }} run-pipeline --help
— the cache is invalidated only when the lockfile changes, not on every code change.
Real-World Example: Deploying a Feature Engineering Pipeline
Consider a pipeline that runs hourly on Airflow, reads raw events from S3, computes user-level features, and writes them to a feature store.

feature-pipeline/
├── pyproject.toml          # abstract deps, project metadata
├── poetry.lock             # committed, pinned lockfile
├── Dockerfile
├── src/
│   └── feature_pipeline/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── main.py
│       ├── readers.py      # S3 reading with boto3 + pandas
│       ├── features.py     # scikit-learn transformers
│       └── writers.py      # writes to Redis feature store
├── tests/
└── .github/workflows/ci.yml

[tool.poetry.dependencies]
python = "^3.11"
pandas = "^2.1"
pyarrow = "^14.0"   # parquet support
boto3 = "^1.34"
scikit-learn = "^1.3"
redis = "^5.0"
pydantic = "^2.5"   # config validation

docker build \
  --build-arg POETRY_VERSION=1.7.1 \
  --label "git-sha=${GITHUB_SHA}" \
  --label "pipeline-version=$(poetry version -s)" \
  -t "${ECR_REGISTRY}/feature-pipeline:${GITHUB_SHA}" \
  .
docker push "${ECR_REGISTRY}/feature-pipeline:${GITHUB_SHA}"

from airflow.providers.cncf.kubernetes.operators.kubernetes_pod import KubernetesPodOperator

feature_task = KubernetesPodOperator(
    task_id="compute_features",
    image=f"{ECR_REGISTRY}/feature-pipeline:{IMAGE_TAG}",  # pinned SHA
    cmds=["run-pipeline"],
    arguments=["--date", "{{ ds }}", "--env", "production"],
    env_vars={
        "DATABASE_URL": Secret("env", "DATABASE_URL", "pipeline-secrets"),
        "S3_BUCKET": "prod-feature-data",
    },
    resources={"request_memory": "2Gi", "request_cpu": "1"},
)
Each Airflow DAG run launches a Kubernetes pod from an immutable Docker image tagged with the git SHA. The environment is identical to what passed CI. The
injects Airflow's execution date for idempotent backfills.
The lockfile must be in version control. If only
run may produce a different resolved set as new versions are released to PyPI.
without hash verification allows silent substitution of packages.
is the source of abstract constraints; the lockfile is where pinning happens. Over-constraining
makes it extremely difficult to update dependencies later.
Not separating dev and production dependencies.
in the production image increases its size, its attack surface, and its startup time.
while developing on 3.11 allows the pipeline to be installed on 3.8 where it may use syntax or APIs unavailable in that version. Be specific:
Rebuilding Docker layers on every code change.
means any code change invalidates the dependency cache layer, adding minutes to each CI build.
is a mutable tag. Pinning to a git SHA ensures that what CI tests is exactly what gets deployed, with no possibility of drift.
must be in git and updated intentionally, never auto-updated by CI runs.
Use hash verification in production installs.
ensures each downloaded artifact matches its expected SHA-256, preventing supply chain attacks where a package registry is compromised.
Keep the builder stage fat (with compilers) and the runtime stage slim. Target 200MB or less for typical pipelines.
Pin Python version at the minor level.
in Dockerfile. Patch version updates to Python should be intentional upgrades, not surprises.
Use Dependabot or Renovate to open automated pull requests when dependencies release security patches. Never let a lockfile go more than 3 months without review.
Test with the exact production image.
CI should build the Docker image and run tests inside it, not just in a local virtual environment. Discrepancies between the venv and the container are a frequent source of production-only bugs.
file in the project root with
automatically switch to this version when entering the directory, ensuring developer machines use the same Python as production.

Shared ETL utilities should be versioned packages on a private registry, not git submodules or copy-pasted code. This allows each pipeline to pin to a specific version of the shared library independently.
PyPI has historically been a target for typosquatting (packages named
) and maintainer account compromise. Hash verification in lockfiles ensures that even if a package is replaced on PyPI, the installed artifact matches what was tested. Tools like
scan installed packages against CVE databases.

pip install pip-audit
pip-audit -r requirements.txt
# or with Poetry:
poetry run pip-audit
credentials into Docker images. Inject secrets at runtime via environment variables, Kubernetes Secrets, or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Use
to verify no secrets are embedded in image layers.
. A compromised pipeline container running as root has full write access to the container filesystem and can potentially escape to the host. Always add a
Use official Python images from Docker Hub or a trusted internal registry. Avoid community base images with unknown contents. Pin base images to a digest, not just a tag:
is re-tagged to point to a different image, builds continue using the exact same base.

A well-packaged pipeline treats reproducibility as a first-class requirement: every dependency is locked, every artifact is hashed, every environment is containerized, and the path from source code to a running production job is automated, auditable, and repeatable.
